one_line_takeaway: TCR is a lightweight (0.3% params), plug-and-play RAG add-on that separates semantic relevance from factual consistency and adds a self-answerability signal via SNR-weighted soft prompts, improving conflict detection, misleading-context robustness, and interpretability—useful against noisy/misinformation-laden retrieval but not against an adaptive adversary.
key_finding: Disentangling semantic vs factual signals and feeding them as SNR-weighted soft prompts improves conflict detection and end-to-end factuality while adding only 0.3% parameters. On Wikidata-Conflict-5K (Llama-3-8B) TCR reaches 84.3 F1 / 0.901 AUROC, beating the best baseline IRCAN (79.1 / 0.845). It loses only 7.2 EM points under 30% distractor injection (vs 9.5 for RAAT, 18.4 for Prompt) and gets the best cross-domain transfer F1 (55.8 Bio / 52.7 Fin). Authors report TCR is best in 22/24 ConflictTQA/PQA cells, improving KGRR by +21.4 and cutting MCOR by -29.3 vs prompting; on single-page Natural Questions it beats Astute RAG by +3.6 pt without fine-tuning. Interpretability results are notable: a clean self-answerability phase transition governs when the model trusts memory vs context (flip rate ~41% for answerability <0.3 vs ~4.7% for >0.7, threshold consistent across all three backbones), and conflict scores align with human labels (avg rho=0.69, kappa=0.66).
core_contribution: The paper introduces TCR (Transparent Conflict Resolution), a plug-and-play RAG module that makes an LLM's handling of knowledge conflicts observable and steerable via three interpretable scalar signals. Its OWN novel components (distinct from the Related-Work taxonomy it cites) are: (1) two separate encoders (semantic Esem and factual Efact, both built on the SFR retrieval model) trained with independent contrastive losses on a purpose-built conflict dataset, so that 'what a sentence is about' (semantic similarity) is disentangled from 'whether its fact holds' (factual consistency); (2) a self-answerability signal sigma_ans estimated from the model's own representations (following Slobodkin et al. 2023) to gauge confidence in parametric memory; and (3) a conflict-aware generation stage that projects the three scalars [sigma_sem, sigma_fact, sigma_ans] through MLP projectors into soft-prompt tokens prepended to the input, with the signals' training contributions dynamically weighted by a Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) softmax. The authors also contribute a theoretical excess-EM-error bound (Theorem 1) that decomposes error under noisy retrieval into a missed-conflict term (rho*alpha) plus conflict-branch error terms, concluding that low false-negative rate (FNR) on conflict detection is the dominant factor. Only ~20 soft-prompt tokens and two small MLP projectors are trained; the backbone stays frozen.
threat_model: The paper is primarily a RAG reliability/factuality paper, but its robustness experiments adopt a security-adjacent threat model that justifies a security tag: the retrieved context is treated as partially untrusted and can contain noise, distractors, misinformation, or content that contradicts correct parametric knowledge (a retrieval-poisoning / misinformation frame). Concretely, the adversary/environment (i) injects distractor passages (30% distractor injection stress test), and (ii) supplies misleading context that could override a correct internal answer. TCR's defensive goals are to raise knowledge-gap recovery (KGRR: correctly using external evidence when memory is wrong) and to cut misleading-context overrides (MCOR: being wrongly flipped by bad context). This is not a formal adversarial-ML threat model (no gradient-based attacker, no worst-case guarantees against an adaptive adversary); the 'attacks' are noisy/misleading retrieval rather than optimized poisoning. I did not invent a stronger threat model than the paper supports.
limitations: 1) Security scope is limited: the 'robustness' is to random/misleading distractors, not to an adaptive or optimized poisoning attacker; no worst-case or certified guarantees, and Theorem 1 assumes query-independent i.i.d. error rates. 2) Heavy reliance on synthetic, self-constructed benchmarks (Wikidata-Conflict-5K, ConflictTQA, ConflictPQA) generated with GPT-4o, which risks distributional overlap between train and eval and possible GPT-4o labeling artifacts. 3) The conflict detector uses a fixed decision boundary (x>0.65, y<0.40) and Fig. 5 shows residual missed conflicts clustering just below the factual threshold plus a false-positive band, so per the paper's own bound the crucial low-FNR condition is not fully met. 4) Detection depends on encoder quality (SFR-based) and a single linear-probe-style separation that may not transfer to long, multi-fact, or multi-hop documents. 5) Human-alignment evaluation is small (300 annotated queries; kappa lowest for Qwen3-8B at 0.61). 6) Some reported gains are modest on real-world NQ (e.g., +0.2 pt over Astute RAG on Llama-3-13B: 56.8 vs 56.6).

datasets_used: Wikidata-Conflict-5K (authors' own conflict-detection benchmark; 5k Wikidata triples, GPT-4o expanded into paraphrase/contradiction/irrelevant surface forms)
ConflictTQA (authors' own, TriviaQA-based; each question paired with golden/irrelevant/conflicting context)
Single-document Natural Questions setting (keeps only top-1 Google result as context)
Exploratory-analysis probe set: 5k Wikidata factual triples embedded with all-MiniLM-L6-v2 and E5-large-v2
quantitative_results: Conflict detection on Wikidata-Conflict-5K (Llama-3-8B): TCR F1 84.3 / AUROC 0.901; TCR+CD2 83.8 / 0.905; best baseline IRCAN 79.1 / 0.845; Prompt 71.2 / 0.779; RAAT 66.4 / 0.732
EM drop under 30% distractor injection (lower better): TCR 7.2, TCR+RAAT 6.1, RAAT 9.5, IRCAN 12.3, Prompt 18.4
Cross-domain transfer F1 (Bio/Fin): TCR 55.8/52.7, TCR+RAAT 56.3/53.1, best baseline IRCAN 52.1/48.9, Prompt 46.7/44.1
ConflictTQA/PQA (authors' prose summary): TCR best in 22/24 cells, +21.4 KGRR and -29.3 MCOR vs prompting; TCR+CD2 achieves lowest MCOR

Natural Questions single-top-1-page accuracy (%): TCR 54.8 / 56.8 / 53.7 for Llama-3-8B / Llama-3-13B / Qwen3-8B; Astute RAG 51.2 / 56.6 / 49.7; InstructRAG 47.8 / 51.2 / 50.3; Self-Route 46.5 / 49.2 / 48.5 (TCR reported +3.6 over Astute, +6.1 over Self-Route, +4.0 largest gain on Qwen3-8B)
Efficiency (A100-80GB): TCR extra params 21M (0.3%), VRAM 18.1 GB, 26.7 tok/s (~94% of Prompt's 28.3 tok/s); RAAT 100M (1.2%), IRCAN 52M (0.6%), Parenting 68M (0.8%)
Human alignment (300 queries): avg Pearson rho=0.69, kappa=0.66 (Llama-3-8B 0.71/0.67; Llama-3-13B 0.74/0.69; Qwen3-8B 0.63/0.61)

Self-answerability flip rate (memory->context override): ~41.3% for answerability in [0,0.3) (N=426), 16.6% for [0.3,0.7) (N=1876), 4.7% for [0.7,1] (N=698); global avg 17.4%; threshold identical across all three backbones
Ablations: removing self-answerability drops MCOR/KGRR ~18/26 pp; fixed (non-SNR) weighting degrades ~17/20 pp; removing signal integration collapses ~55/53 pp
Signal-trajectory heuristic: factual > self-answerability before decoding step 10 predicts success in ~70% of cases
baselines_compared: RAAT (Fang et al. 2024, adaptive adversarial training)
InstructRAG (Wei et al. 2025) and Self-Route (Xu et al. 2024a) on real-world NQ
TCR ablations (-semantic, -factual, -SNR, hard-prompt) and hybrids (TCR+CD2, TCR+IRCAN, TCR+RAAT)
